title: Site Suitability Model for Utility Scale Solar Farms in BC
text: This project utilizes Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA) to evaluate potential locations for utility-scale (1MW+) solar development across British Columbia. By integrating environmental, technical, and economic variables, the study identifies optimal development zones while strictly adhering to provincial land-use regulations.

title: Site Suitability Model for Utility Scale Solar Farms in BC
text:
The Suitability Framework
The model balances development potential against conservation priorities through a weighted overlay of key criteria:







Technical & Economic Drivers
Environmental & Regulatory Constraints




Solar Power: Global Horizontal Irradiance (GHI)
Terrain Constraints: Water Bodies, Wetlands, Permanent Snow and Ice


Terrain: Slope and aspect optimization
Regulatory: Agricultural Land Reserve (ALR), Protected and Conserved Areas


Infrastructure: Proximity to Substations and Major Roads
Land Cover: Dense Forests (Canopy>40%), Artificial/Built Environments


Demand: Proximity to Major Cities
Buffers: Roads (100m) and urban (1000m) setback

title: Site Suitability Model for Utility Scale Solar Farms in BC
text:
Technical Workflow & Implementation
To translate these diverse datasets into a unified suitability model, I developed a custom end-to-end spatial workflow in R:

Data Harmonization: Standardized heterogeneous datasets—ranging from vector land-use boundaries to raster-based solar irradiance—into a common 30m grid aligned with the BC Environmental Albers (EPSG:3005) projection.
Constraint Modeling: Programmatically generated exclusion masks for protected areas, high-canopy forests, and hydrologic features, applying precise Euclidean distance buffers for urban and infrastructure setbacks.
Criteria Weight Derivation: Employed the Analytic Hierarchy Process to derive objective influence weights using relative importance ratings from industry experts. By constructing a pairwise comparison matrix and calculating the principal eigenvector, I ensured a Consistency Ratio (CR) < 0.1, mathematically validating the relative importance of solar potential versus technical constraints.
Weighted Overlay Analysis: Integrated the AHP-derived weights into a multi-criteria scoring system. This reclassified model input criteria into a common scale, allowing for the quantitative comparison of technical potential against environmental sensitivity.

Calculated Suitability Weights








Criterion
Derived Weight
Primary Justification




Global Horizontal Irradiance (GHI)
26.8
The fundamental driver of project energy generation potential


Proximity to Energy Infrastructure
25.9
Influence on grid optimization and installation costs


Proximity to Major Roads
24.8
Increases site accessibility and facilitates construction


Aspect
10.1
Effects solar radiation incidence


Proximity to Major Cities
9.0
Benefits of reduced distribution costs are moderated by social preference for developments away from population centers


Slope
3.3
Simplified installation process on gentle terrain

title: Site Suitability Model for Utility Scale Solar Farms in BC
text:
Interactive Suitability Map
Suitability is ranked on a 1-5 scale, with 1 being least suitable and 5 being very suitable. The mask layer, ranked as 0, represents unsuitable land where solar farm installations are not feasible or permitted.

title: Site Suitability Model for Utility Scale Solar Farms in BC
text:
Aspatial Results





Suitability Distribution Across BC’s Total Land Base


76.5% (723,947.99 km²) of BC is restricted from solar farm development. Non-restricted land
accounts for the remaining 23.55% (223,027.33 km²) of the province. Within this area, class 1
comprises 3.86% of the provincial land base, class 2 represents the largest share at 16.21%, and
class 3 accounts for 3.25%. The highest suitability classes occupy only a very small portion of the
province, with class 4 covering 0.21% and class 5 covering just 0.005%.






Areal Extent Occupied Per Suitability Class


Areal extent (km2) of each suitability class within the non-restricted land base. Class 2 occupies the largest area at 153,533.01 km2, while class 5 occupies the smallest area at 51.50km2. Class 1, 3, and 4 occupy varying areas and comprise the remaining 69,477.23 km2 of land available for solar farm installations.

title: Investigating Species at Risk Observations Within Canadian National Parks
text:
Species at risk (SAR), particularly those with specialized habitat requirements, are highly vulnerable to landscape change driven by human activity and development. Protected areas are intended to mitigate these pressures by conserving critical habitats and maintaining biodiversity. However, while national parks encompass a range of ecosystems, certain habitats essential to SAR may be underrepresented within park boundaries and therefore inadequately protected. This national-scale analysis evaluates which SAR occur within Canadian national parks and assesses whether their associated habitats are sufficiently represented. For this study, the analysis focused on terrestrial mammals, with all data processing conducted in Python using Pandas, GeoPandas, and Matplotlib.
Project Co-creators: Kenzie Stevenson, Asiya Rizva

Data

Canadian Provincial Park Polygons
SAR Registry (as defined under the Species at Risk Act)
GBIF (Global Biodiversity Information Facility) Species Occurrence Data



Objective 1: Identify the spatial occurrence of species at risk in Canadian National Parks
Click the drop downs to view code






Clean SAR data





# Filter sar_df to only include terrestrial mammals. This cuts down the time taken for this analysis.
sar_df_filtered = sar_df[sar_df["taxonomic_group"] == "Mammals (terrestrial)"]

# Create list of ALL sar species names from the sar_df_filtered file
all_species_list = list(sar_df_filtered.iloc[:, 3])

# Remove duplicates from the species list.
tar_species = list(set(all_species_list))

# Check that the tar species have been kept
print(tar_species)

# Inspect the length to get an idea of expected computation time.
len(tar_species)









Pull GBIF data





# Adapted from Seely https://github.com/subornaa/Geospatial-data-analysis-project-started-kit/blob/main/parks_sar.ipynb
# Based on code from https://discourse.gbif.org/t/retrieving-inaturalist-observations-for-a-region-observers-with-pygbif/4565

species_occurrences = {}

# Adapted into a loop to download observations for each species in our target list.
for TARGET_SP in tar_species:
    # Indicate which species is being downloaded
    print(f"\nDownloading occurrences for {TARGET_SP}...")

    # Create empty list for results
    results = []
    offset = 0
    # If occurrences are for TARGET_SP, in Canada, have coordinates and no geospatial issues, assign them to out
    while True:
        out = occurrences.search(scientificName=TARGET_SP, # this seaches the GBIF database for all observations of the species in the loop.
                                 country='CA', # this narrows down the scope to Canada
                                 hasCoordinate=True,
                                 hasGeospatialIssue=False,
                                 offset=offset
                                )
        # If there are no results of occurrences, break
        if len(out['results']) == 0:
            break
        # Give an indication of progress
        results.extend(out['results'])
        offset += len(out['results'])
        print(f"Total records downloaded: {len(results)}")

    # If no reslts, continue
    if not results:
        print(f"---No data found for {TARGET_SP}")
        continue

    lat_ls = [results[i]['decimalLatitude'] for i in range(len(results))]
    lon_ls = [results[i]['decimalLongitude'] for i in range(len(results))]

    # Create dataframe of results for this species.
    df = pd.DataFrame(results)

    # Append results for this species to the occurrences_gdf
    occurrences_gdf = gpd.GeoDataFrame(
        df,
        geometry=gpd.points_from_xy(df.decimalLongitude, df.decimalLatitude),
        # Indicate df crs
        crs="EPSG:4326"
        # Transform to parks_gdf crs
    ).to_crs(parks_gdf.crs)

    # Keep only points inside parks using geopandas spatial join with intersects predicate
    in_parks_gdf = gpd.sjoin(occurrences_gdf, parks_gdf, predicate='intersects')

    # Store results of species occurrences in the parks
    species_occurrences[TARGET_SP] = in_parks_gdf

    print(f"---Points inside parks: {len(in_parks_gdf)}")




Visual Results



Terrestrial mammals occurrence data plotting within Canadian Parks using GBIF and SAR Registry.





Objective 2 Analyze conservation value of the National Parks
Click the drop downs to view code






Calculate index that represents conservation value of each park





# Create a dataframe with values based on the SARA listing level of each species found in each park 
# These values were subjectively selected and could be adapted based on further research.
# Higher risk species given a higher weight than lower risk species

rank_value_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "sara_status": ["Endangered", "Threatened", "Special Concern"],
    "sara_value": [7, 3, 1]
})

# Merge the sar_df with the rank_value_df to have a SARA value for each species
    # Merge is the geopandas attribute join, as opposed to s.join which is the spatial join
sar_df_vals = sar_df_filtered.merge(rank_value_df, on="sara_status", how="left")

# Keep only relevant SARA fields
sara_info = (sar_df_vals[["species", "sara_status", "sara_value"]])

# Reset index (drop the old index so we don't get duplicate columns)
all_sp_occ = all_sp_occ.reset_index(drop=True)

# Merge SARA info into occurrences, add SARA_info (all relevant fields) to all occurrences
all_occ = all_sp_occ.merge(sara_info, on="species", how="left")

# Drop any duplicates of the same species and park combination
unique_species_per_park = all_occ.drop_duplicates(subset=["NAME_E", "species"])

# Counts the number of unique species per park
summary = (
    # Start with DataFrame
    unique_species_per_park
    # Group by unique park name and endangered status
    .groupby(["NAME_E", "sara_status"])
    # Count the number of species in each park/sara status category
    .agg(n_species=("species", "count"))
    .reset_index()
)

print("National Parks and number of occurring listed species: \n", summary)

Assign final weighted score to each park





# Merge the DataFrames to have SARA_values from rank_value_df
summary_with_values = summary.merge(rank_value_df, on="sara_status", how="left")

# Create new column that calculates the final weighted score for each park
# Weighted score is calculated as the number of species multiplied by the weight of that listing level summed for the park.
summary_with_values["weighted_score"] = summary_with_values["n_species"] * summary_with_values["sara_value"]


# Create summary table with Park Scores
park_scores = (
    # Start with the DataFrame
    summary_with_values
    # Group by unique name and weighted score, sum and sort
    .groupby("NAME_E")["weighted_score"]
    .sum()
    .reset_index(name="weighted_score")
    .sort_values("weighted_score", ascending=False)
)

park_scores2 = park_scores.copy()
# Clean up National Park Names
park_scores2['NAME_E']=park_scores2['NAME_E'].str.replace('NATIONAL PARK OF CANADA', '')
park_scores2['NAME_E']=park_scores2['NAME_E'].str.replace('NATIONAL PARK RESERVE OF CANADA', '')

print("The top 5 parks with highest conservation value are \n", park_scores2.head(5))

Objective 3: Identify species with underrepresented habitat in parks
Click the drop downs to view code






Use point buffer as indicator of species core habitat





# Make sure CRS of two input layers match
all_sp_occ = all_sp_occ.to_crs(parks_gdf.crs)

# Create 5000 m buffer around each species observation point
    # We selected a large buffer to capture potential habitat for each species. A large buffer reduces the impact of many occurrences on the percieved habitat size.
    # In the full analysis, we would utilize available landcover layers, and available range information to indicate what is truly "available habitat".
all_sp_occ['buffer'] = all_sp_occ.geometry.buffer(5000)

# Set buffer as the active geometry column
all_sp_occ = all_sp_occ.set_geometry('buffer')

# Dissolve buffers so area is not double counted for occurences with overlapping buffers
buffers_dissolved = all_sp_occ.dissolve(by='species', as_index = False)

# Intersect dissolved species buffers with parks polygon layer
buffers_in_parks = gpd.overlay(buffers_dissolved, parks_gdf, how='intersection')

# Calculate area in km2
buffers_in_parks['area_km2'] = buffers_in_parks.geometry.area / 1_000_000

# Create summary table
species_area_summary = (
    buffers_in_parks
    .groupby('species')['area_km2']
    .sum()
    .reset_index()
    .sort_values(by='area_km2')
)
print("The species with the least area of habitat in the National Parks are: \n", species_area_summary)

The species with the least area of habitat in the National Parks are:

# Sample of mapping all species habitat in one park

targ_park = "BANFF NATIONAL PARK OF CANADA"

# Isolate park boundary
park_boundary = parks_gdf[parks_gdf["NAME_E"] == targ_park]

# extract just target park habitats
targ_buffers = buffers_in_parks[buffers_in_parks["NAME_E_2"] == targ_park]

    ax=ax,

title: Individual Tree Segmentation using LiDAR Data from Malcolm Knapp Research Forest
text: Individual tree segmentation is a cornerstone of modern forestry, transforming how we monitor and manage forest ecosystems. By enabling automated analysis at the single-tree level, this technique provides precise estimates of vital structural parameters (biomass and carbon stocks) that would otherwise require time and labor intensive fieldwork. Ultimately, these insights empower more informed and sustainable forest management. For this study, the lidR package was used to evaluate two primary segmentation methods on four different plots within the Malcolm Knapp Research Forest in British Columbia. Point cloud normalization and filtering was completed prior to segmentation using a point-cloud derived Digital Elevation Model (DEM).

title: Individual Tree Segmentation using LiDAR Data from Malcolm Knapp Research Forest
text:
Method 1
Point Cloud: Segmentation was based solely on the normalized point cloud data. Li et al., 2012 (Li2012) algorithm was used.
#Plot 1:
plot_1_seg <- segment_trees(mkrf_plot_1, li2012(dt1 = 1.5, dt2 = 2, R = 2, Zu = 15, hmin = 2, speed_up = 10))
plot(plot_1_seg, color = "treeID")

#Plot 2:
plot_2_seg <- segment_trees(mkrf_plot_2, li2012(dt1 = 1.5, dt2 = 2, R = 2, Zu = 15, hmin = 2, speed_up = 10))
plot(plot_2_seg, color = "treeID")

#Plot 3
plot_3_seg <- segment_trees(mkrf_plot_3, li2012(dt1 = 1.5, dt2 = 2, R = 2, Zu = 15, hmin = 2, speed_up = 10))
plot(plot_3_seg, color = "treeID")

#Plot 4
plot_4_seg <- segment_trees(mkrf_plot_4, li2012(dt1 = 1.5, dt2 = 2, R = 2, Zu = 15, hmin = 2, speed_up = 10))
plot(plot_4_seg, color = "treeID")

title: Individual Tree Segmentation using LiDAR Data from Malcolm Knapp Research Forest
text:
Results






Plot 1

title: Individual Tree Segmentation using LiDAR Data from Malcolm Knapp Research Forest
text:
Method 2
Canopy Height Model (CHM): Segmentation is based on a CHM using the tree-centric approach. Dalponte and Coomes (dalponte2016) algorithm was used.
#Plot 1:
chm_plot1 <- rasterize_canopy(mkrf_plot_1, 0.5, pitfree(thresholds = c(0, 10, 20, 30), max_edge = c(0,1), subcircle = 0.2))
tree_tops1 <- locate_trees(chm_plot1, lmf(ws = 5, hmin = 2))
crowns_plot1 <- segment_trees(mkrf_plot_1, dalponte2016(chm_plot1, tree_tops1, th_tree = 2, th_seed = 0.45, th_cr = 0.55, max_cr = 10, ID = "treeID"))
plot(crowns_plot1, color = "treeID")

#Plot 2:
chm_plot2 <- rasterize_canopy(mkrf_plot_2, 0.5, pitfree(thresholds = c(0, 10, 20, 30),max_edge = c(0,1), subcircle = 0.2))
tree_tops2 <- locate_trees(chm_plot2, lmf(ws = 5, hmin = 2))
crowns_plot2 <- segment_trees(mkrf_plot_2, dalponte2016(chm_plot2, tree_tops2, th_tree = 2, th_seed = 0.45, th_cr = 0.55, max_cr = 10, ID = "treeID"))
plot(crowns_plot2, color = "treeID")

#Plot 3:
chm_plot3 <- rasterize_canopy(mkrf_plot_3, 0.5, pitfree(thresholds = c(0, 10, 20, 30), max_edge = c(0,1), subcircle = 0.2))
tree_tops3 <- locate_trees(chm_plot3, lmf(ws = 5, hmin = 2))
crowns_plot3 <- segment_trees(mkrf_plot_3, dalponte2016(chm_plot3, tree_tops3, th_tree = 2, th_seed = 0.45, th_cr = 0.55, max_cr = 10, ID = "treeID"))
plot(crowns_plot3, color = "treeID")

#Plot 4:
chm_plot4 <- rasterize_canopy(mkrf_plot_4, 0.5, pitfree(thresholds = c(0, 10, 20, 30), max_edge = c(0,1), subcircle = 0.2))
tree_tops4 <- locate_trees(chm_plot4, lmf(ws = 5, hmin = 2))
crowns_plot4 <- segment_trees(mkrf_plot_4, dalponte2016(chm_plot4, tree_tops4, th_tree = 2, th_seed = 0.45, th_cr = 0.55, max_cr = 10, ID = "treeID"))
plot(crowns_plot4, color = "treeID")

title: Map Gallery
text:
Click any map to view full size!







Fuel Related Variable Prediction in a Post-Burn Scenario Using Machine Learning
This map displays the prediction of crown closure and fuel type using the random forest algorithm. The analysis included experimenting with different predictor variables, sampling types (random, stratified, fixed point), and algorithm parameters. An evaluation of model performance was done to determine the best model and produce the resulting surfaces.











Terrain and Riparian Management Area Analysis
This map visualizes the Nahmint watershed and riparian management areas. This analysis involved mapping the stream network using a DEM and extracting stream characteristics (order, gradient, fish bearing potential) to quantify appropriate riparian reserve and management zones buffers.











Salmon Stream Network Analysis
This hydrology based analysis applied network topology to evaluate a capability model of salmon habitat along stream segments using linear referencing and barriers. Accessible and inaccessible salmon habitat was then visualized and quantified.











Spatial Interpolation and Visualization of LiDAR Data
This layout provides a side-by-side comparison of different spatial interpolation algorithms. Zonal statistics present differences in mean and standard deviation between elevation and slope rasters produced with Spline, Nearest Neighbour, and Kriging across three different terrain zones.











Manual Digitization Accuracy Assessment
This map presents an accuracy assessment and visual comparison of manually digitized building footprints to reference data. Kart was utilized for spatial version control.











Least Cost Path Analysis
This analysis determines the most efficient routes for Grizzly Bears across a complex landscape, accounting for ‘costs’ such as steep terrain, land cover, and road crossings.











Cartographic Modelling of Old Growth Forest Inventories
A spatial assessment of old-growth forest patches. This map compares the percent of old growth forest, derived from VRI, to provincial targets. It highlights conservation priorities based on forest age and fragmentation metrics.

title: Mackenzie Thomson
text:
Master’s Student in Geomatics for Environmental Management
With a Bachelor’s degree in Geography and hands-on experience as a forestry field technician, I have a deep appreciation for the complexity of British Columbia’s landscapes. Currently pursuing a Master’s in Geomatics for Environmental Management (MGEM) at UBC, I’m turning that field experience into technical expertise. I’m passionate about leveraging geospatial technologies to better understand and manage our changing environments. Through the MGEM program, I’m honing my skills in spatial modeling, remote sensing, and geospatial data analysis to tackle complex environmental challenges.

title: Analysis of Fire Burn Severity and Post-Fire Vegetation Recovery with Landsat
text:
The summer of 2017 was one of the most severe fire seasons in British Columbia, including the Prouton Lakes fire near Williams Lake. This project quantified burn severity and tracked post-fire vegetation recovery using multi-temporal Landsat 8 OLI imagery. I processed large volumes of imagery by developing custom functions and implementing iterative workflows to efficiently calculate burn severity and vegetation indices and monitor landscape-scale recovery patterns over multiple years.
Check out some code snippets:






Landsat imagery processing and vegetation indices function creation





#(1)for each L2 product, put each surface reflectance band (Band 1 to Band 7) in the same raster object

#read in directory as list
dirlist <- list.dirs("data/Landsat 8 OLI_TIRS C2 L2", full.names = TRUE, recursive = FALSE)
dirlist[1:5]

#create list to store all tifs
bands <- list() 
#for loop to read in all files from directory matching pattern
for (i in seq_along(dirlist)) {
  tif_files <- list.files(
    dirlist[i],
    pattern = "SR_B[1-7]\\.TIF$",
    full.names = TRUE,
    recursive = TRUE
  )
  #order them 1-7
  tif_files <- tif_files[order(tif_files)]
  #store as rast object
  bands[[i]] <- rast(tif_files)
}
bands[1:5] #list of SR rasters with bands 1-7 grouped

#(2)mask pixels that do not have the value 21824 (clear conditions) in the QA_PIXEL layer using function 

#load in QA_PIXELS
qa_files <- list()
for (i in seq_along(dirlist)) {
  qa_list <- list.files(
    dirlist[i], 
    pattern = "QA_PIXEL\\.TIF$", 
    full.names = TRUE, 
    recursive = TRUE
  )
  #store as rast object
  qa_files[[i]] <- rast(qa_list)
}
qa_files[1:5] #list of QA rasters

#create function to mask band raster using QA raster
mask_fun <- function(band_rast, qa_rast) {
  good <- qa_rast == 21824 
  masked <- terra::mask(band_rast, good, maskvalues = FALSE) #like inverse
  return(masked)
}

#use function in mapply to iterate over list
masked_bands <- mapply(
  mask_fun,
  band_rast = bands,
  qa_rast = qa_files,
  SIMPLIFY = FALSE
)
masked_bands

#(3)crop the multi-layer raster to the Prouton Lakes fire extent
#isolate Prouton Lakes
prouton_extent <- raw_fire %>%
  filter(FIRE_NO == "C30870") 
prouton_extent <- st_geometry(prouton_extent)
plot(prouton_extent)

#transform polygon to raster CRS
prouton_extent_proj <- st_transform(prouton_extent, crs(masked_bands[[1]]))

#save cropped rasters to SR folder
cropped_output <- file.path("output", "LC08_L2SP_048023_SR")

#get basenames
product_ids <- basename(dirlist)

for (i in seq_along(masked_bands)) {
  in_rasts <- masked_bands[[i]]
  cropped <- crop(in_rasts, prouton_extent_proj)
  names(cropped) <- names(in_rasts)
  #save to correct subfolder
  cropped_file <- file.path(cropped_output, paste0(product_ids[i], "_SR.tif"))
  writeRaster(cropped, filename = cropped_file, overwrite = TRUE)
}

#list all cropped rasters
cropped_files <- list.files(cropped_output, pattern = "\\.tif$", full.names = TRUE)
cropped_files

#read each raster into a list
masked_bands_prouton <- lapply(cropped_files, function(f) terra::rast(f))

#(4)calculate NDVI for each cropped and masked raster
ndvi_output <- file.path("output", "LC08_L2SP_048023_NDVI")
ndvi_list <- vector("list", length(masked_bands_prouton))

#create function
ndvi_calc <- function(rast, product) {
  red <- rast[[4]]
  nir <- rast[[5]]
  ndvi <- (nir - red) / (nir + red)
  ndvi_file <- file.path(ndvi_output, paste0(product, "_NDVI.tif"))
  writeRaster(ndvi, filename = ndvi_file, overwrite = TRUE)
  
  return(ndvi)
}

#loop through all cropped+masked rasters
for (i in seq_along(masked_bands_prouton)) {
  rast <- masked_bands_prouton[[i]]
  product_id <- product_ids[i]
  ndvi_list[[i]] <- ndvi_calc(rast, product_id)
}

#(5)calculate NBR for each cropped and masked raster
nbr_output <- file.path("output", "LC08_L2SP_048023_NBR")
nbr_list <- vector("list", length(masked_bands_prouton))

#create function
nbr_calc <- function(rast, product) {
  nir <- rast[[5]]
  swir2 <- rast[[7]]
  nbr <- (nir - swir2) / (nir + swir2)
  nbr_file <- file.path(nbr_output, paste0(product, "_NBR.tif"))
  writeRaster(nbr, filename = nbr_file, overwrite = TRUE)
  
  return(nbr)
}

#loop through all cropped+masked rasters
for (i in seq_along(masked_bands_prouton)) {
  rast <- masked_bands_prouton[[i]]
  product_id <- product_ids[i]
  nbr_list[[i]] <- nbr_calc(rast, product_id)
}

#(6)plot TCC of area before and after fire
sr_files <- list.files("output/LC08_L2SP_048023_SR", pattern = "_SR.tif$", full.names = TRUE)

#find july 7 2015
before_fire <- sr_files[grepl("20150707", sr_files)]

#find july 15 2018
after_fire <- sr_files[grepl("20180715", sr_files)]

#load as spatrast
before_fire_rast <- rast(before_fire)
after_fire_rast <- rast(after_fire)

#plot side by side
par(mfrow = c(1,2))
terra::plotRGB(before_fire_rast, r=4, g=3, b=2, 
               stretch = "lin",
               main = "Before Fire")
terra::plotRGB(after_fire_rast, r=4, g=3, b=2,
               stretch = "lin",
               main = "Before Fire")









NDVI and NBR yearly composite creation





#(1)get only NDVI rasts in July and August
ndvi_files <- list.files("output/LC08_L2SP_048023_NDVI", pattern="_NDVI.tif$", full.names = TRUE)

#extract basename
ndvi_names <- basename(ndvi_files)

#extract date section of file name
date_extract_ndvi <- str_extract(ndvi_names, "\\d{8}") #first chunk of 8 numbers 
ndvi_dates <- as.Date(date_extract_ndvi, format="%Y%m%d")

#filter by July and August
july_aug_idx_ndvi <- format(ndvi_dates, "%m") %in% c("07", "08")
july_aug_ndvi <- ndvi_files[july_aug_idx_ndvi]

#get corresponding years for July/August images
ndvi_years <- format(ndvi_dates[july_aug_idx_ndvi], "%Y") #all years (includes repeats)
unique_years_ndvi <- sort(unique(ndvi_years))  #only unique years (no repeats)
unique_years_ndvi

#(2)create yearly composites of average NDVI
ndvi_per_year <- lapply(unique_years_ndvi, function(y) {
  files_ndvi <- july_aug_ndvi[ndvi_years == y]
  year_stack_ndvi <- rast(files_ndvi)
  app(year_stack_ndvi, fun = mean, na.rm = TRUE)
})
ndvi_per_year

#same for NBR
nbr_files <- list.files("output/LC08_L2SP_048023_NBR", pattern="_NBR.tif$", full.names = TRUE)

#extract basename
nbr_names <- basename(nbr_files)

#extract date section
date_extract_nbr <- str_extract(nbr_names, "\\d{8}")
nbr_dates <- as.Date(date_extract_nbr, format="%Y%m%d")

#filter by July and August
july_aug_idx_nbr <- format(nbr_dates, "%m") %in% c("07", "08")
july_aug_nbr <- nbr_files[july_aug_idx_nbr]

#get corresponding years for July/August images
nbr_years <- format(nbr_dates[july_aug_idx_nbr], "%Y")
unique_years_nbr <- sort(unique(nbr_years))

#assign years as the names
#names(nbr_per_year) <- unique_years_nbr

#(3)create yearly composites of average NBR
nbr_per_year <- lapply(unique_years_nbr, function(y) {
  files_nbr <- july_aug_nbr[nbr_years == y]
  year_stack_nbr <- rast(files_nbr)
  app(year_stack_nbr, fun = mean, na.rm = TRUE)
})
nbr_per_year

#(4)calculate average NDVI of all pixels overlaid by PL fire, for each year in the time series
#assign years as the rast names
names(ndvi_per_year) <- unique_years_ndvi 

prouton_vect <- vect(prouton_extent_proj)
ndvi_fire_avg <- lapply(ndvi_per_year, function(r) {
  masked <- mask(r, prouton_vect)       
  mean(values(masked), na.rm = TRUE)   
})

# convert to a data.frame to view
ndvi_fire_avg_df <- data.frame(
  year = names(ndvi_per_year),
  avg_ndvi = unlist(ndvi_fire_avg)
)
ndvi_fire_avg_df

#plot average NDVI across the PL fire area through time
ggplot(ndvi_fire_avg_df, (aes( x = year, y =avg_ndvi, group = 1))) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() 

#plot visually
ndvi_stack <- rast(ndvi_per_year)
plot(ndvi_stack)
 









Burn severity classification





#(1)seperate pre-fire and post-fire 
#remember nbr_per_year = list of nbr mean per year within PL area
pre_fire_nbr <- nbr_per_year[[3]]
post_fire_nbr <- nbr_per_year[[6]]
  
#(2)calculate dNBR
dnbr <- pre_fire_nbr - post_fire_nbr
plot(dnbr)

#(3)reclassify using table provided 
m <- c(-0.2, 0.15, 0,
       0.15, 0.25, 1,
       0.25, 0.3, 2,
       0.3, 1, 3)
dnbr_mat <- matrix(m, ncol = 3, byrow = TRUE)
burn_severity <- terra::classify(x = dnbr, rcl = dnbr_mat, include.lowest = TRUE, right = TRUE)
#make categorical
burn_severity <- as.factor(burn_severity)

#add levels
levels(burn_severity) <- data.frame(id = c(0, 1, 2, 3), label = c("Unburned", "Low Severity", "Medium Severity", "High Severity"))

#(4)plot dnbr rast with PL fire extent on top
plot(burn_severity, col = c("darkgreen","yellow","orange","red"), main="Burn Severity")
#convert SpatVecter to sf
prouton_sf <- st_as_sf(prouton_extent_proj)
#extract geom
prouton_geom <- st_geometry(prouton_sf)
#overlay
plot(prouton_geom, add = TRUE, border = "black")









Post-fire vegetation recovery quantification using NDVI





#(1)dnbr raster to polygons
burn_polys <- terra::as.polygons(burn_severity, dissolve = TRUE)
bp_sf <- st_as_sf(burn_polys)
plot(bp_sf)

#(2)retrieve categorical labels assigned 
burn_polys$burn_class <- levels(burn_severity)[[1]]$label
bp_df <- as.data.frame(burn_polys)
bp_df

#extract years from grand list 
ndvi_rasters <- ndvi_per_year[c("2018", "2019", "2020", "2021")]

#make list to store extract
ndvi_extract_list <- list()

#loop through to assign burn class to each poly
for (bs in 1:nrow(burn_polys)) {
  poly <- burn_polys[bs, ]       
  burn_class_val <- poly$burn_class[1]     
  
  #loop through to extract ndvi values per bs class
  for (i in seq_along(ndvi_rasters)) {
    
    rast_obj <- ndvi_rasters[[i]]
    yr <- names(ndvi_rasters)[i]
    ex <- terra::extract(rast_obj, poly, method = "simple", bind = TRUE)
    #add burn class to extraction and assign burn class values to extracted
    ex$burn_class <- burn_class_val
    #add and assign year
    ex$year <- yr
    #store in list
    ndvi_extract_list <- c(ndvi_extract_list, list(ex))
  }
}
#(4)make into dataframe
ndvi_per_bs <- bind_rows(ndvi_extract_list)

#change mean to ndvi (name stored is spatrast list)
ndvi_per_bs <- ndvi_per_bs %>%
  rename(ndvi = mean)

#(5)plot ndvi composite of each year 
ndvi_years <- list(
  "2018" = ndvi_per_year[["2018"]],
  "2019" = ndvi_per_year[["2019"]],
  "2020" = ndvi_per_year[["2020"]],
  "2021" = ndvi_per_year[["2021"]]
)

#set plot layout
par(mfrow = c(2, 2)) 
#loop over each year to plot
for (yr in names(ndvi_years)) {
  plot(
  ndvi_years[[yr]],
  main = paste("NDVI", yr),   
  range = c(0, 0.5),           
  axes = TRUE
  )
}
 
#(6)boxplot
#make ordered so burn class plots unburned to high burn
ndvi_per_bs$burn_class <- factor(ndvi_per_bs$burn_class, levels = c("Unburned", "Low Severity", "Medium Severity", "High Severity"), ordered = TRUE)

ggplot(ndvi_per_bs, aes(x=year, y=ndvi, fill=burn_class)) + 
  geom_boxplot(position = position_dodge()) +
  labs(fill = "Burn Severity") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("green", "yellow", "orange", "red"))





Results




Image 1: Yearly composite of NDVI averaged across July and August in the Prouton Lakes fire area . Pixels with unclear conditions have been masked using Landsats QA Pixel layer.







Image 2: Prouton Lakes fire burn severity classification using bNBR values, considering 2015 as the pre-fire year and 2018 as the post-fire year. dNBR ranges are as followed: -0.2-0.15 are unburned, 0.15-0.25 are low severity, 0.25-0.3 are medium severity, 0.3-1 are high severity.







Image 3: Post fire vegetation recovery in years following the fire. NDVI values were extracted from each burn severity class in order to understand the distribution of vegetation recovery per class.

title: Leveraging ModelBuilder and Script Tools for Spatial Analysis of North Vancouver Road Network
text:
This project demonstrates an integrated GIS workflow designed to support Municipal Transportation Asset Management reporting for the City and District of North Vancouver. By combining the visual logic of ArcGIS Pro ModelBuilder with Python (ArcPy) scripting, this analysis automates the classification and spatial quantification of road networks.
The primary objective was to transform raw OpenStreetMap (OSM) data into a structured hierarchy, enabling automated variable-distance buffering and summaries of specific road types. This hybrid approach provides a scalable framework for calculating essential infrastructure metrics, such as total segment lengths and buffer areas for primary transit corridors.

title: Leveraging ModelBuilder and Script Tools for Spatial Analysis of North Vancouver Road Network
text:
Data

City and District of North Vancouver Administrative Boundary
City and District of North Vancouver Road Network
OSM Highways for North America

title: Leveraging ModelBuilder and Script Tools for Spatial Analysis of North Vancouver Road Network
text:
Workflow
Hierarchical Classification of Road Network: Open Street Map Highways for North America symbology was applied to North Vancouver’s road network, allowing hierarchical classification of roads using a hierarchical code for each OSM ‘Highway’ category:







OSM ‘Highway’ Category
Hierarchy Code




Motoways
5


Primary and secondary links
4


Residential and tertiary links
3


Service roads and living streets
2


Corridors, cycleways, foot/pedestrian paths, tracks, and constructions
1


Other
0



ModelBuilder Visual:

title: Leveraging ModelBuilder and Script Tools for Spatial Analysis of North Vancouver Road Network
text:
Attribute Driven Variable Buffering: Python scripting in ArcGIS Pro was then used to create a binary road classification based on road hierarchy, then converted to a variable distance buffer.
arcpy.env.overwriteOutput = True
arcpy.env.workspace = r"...\TransportationRoutesAnalysis.gdb"

fc = "NV_RoadsHierarchy"
out_fc = os.path.join(arcpy.env.workspace, "study_area_roads_hierarchy_bufferByRd")

#Field Names
rd_field = "RD_CODE"
buffer_field = "BUFFER_DISTANCE"

#Add RD_CODE field
existing = [f.name.upper() for f in arcpy.ListFields(fc)]
if rd_field not in existing:
    arcpy.management.AddField(fc, rd_field, "LONG")

#Calculate RD_CODE from hierarchy code
rd_expression = "1 if !Hierarchy! >= 4 else 0"
arcpy.management.CalculateField(fc, rd_field, rd_expression, "PYTHON3")

#Add BUFFER_DISTANCE field
if buffer_field not in existing:
    arcpy.management.AddField(fc, buffer_field, "DOUBLE")

#Calculate BUFFER_DISTANCE from RD_CODE
buffer_expression = "!RD_CODE! * 100"

arcpy.management.CalculateField(fc, buffer_field, buffer_expression, "PYTHON3")

#Run variable distance buffer
arcpy.analysis.Buffer(
    in_features=fc,
    out_feature_class=out_fc,
    buffer_distance_or_field=buffer_field,
    line_side="FULL",
    line_end_type="ROUND",
    dissolve_option="NONE",
    method="PLANAR")
Filter and summarize road length: A SearchCursor was then applied to isolate primary routes and calculate the total combined segment length and buffer area.
gdb = r"...\TransportationRoutesAnalysis.gdb"
fc = os.path.join(gdb, 'NV_RoadsHierarchy')
arcpy.env.workspace = gdb

#Filter and sum road lengths using a searchcursor
where_clause = "Hierarchy > 4"
total_length_m = 0.0

with arcpy.da.SearchCursor(fc, ["SHAPE@LENGTH"], where_clause) as cursor:
    for (length_val,) in cursor:
        if length_val is not None:
            total_length_m += length_val

#Print output 
print(f"Total length (Hierarchy > 4): {total_length_m:,.2f} m "
      f"({total_length_m/1000:,.2f} km)")

[1] Total length (Hierarchy > 4): 49,751.07 m (49.75 km)

#Filter and calculate area using a searchcursor
buffer_field = "BUFFER_DISTANCE"
total_buffer_area_m2 = 0.0

with arcpy.da.SearchCursor(fc, ["SHAPE@", buffer_field], where_clause) as cursor:
    for geom, buf_dist in cursor:
        if geom is not None and buf_dist:
            buf_geom = geom.buffer(buf_dist)  
            total_buffer_area_m2 += buf_geom.area

print(f"Total buffer area (Hierarchy > 4/RD_CODE=1): {total_buffer_area_m2:,.2f} m² "
      f"({total_buffer_area_m2/1e6:,.3f} km²)")

[1] Total buffer area (Hierarchy > 4 / RD_CODE=1): 16,211,603.01 m² (16.212 km²)
Visual Results:

title: Time Series Analyses using Satellite Imagery
text: Time series analysis are powerful due to their capabilities in predicting future trends based on historical patterns. Moving beyond static analysis enables proactive decision making and better resource planning. Two separate time series analysis were undergone:

title: Time Series Analyses using Satellite Imagery
section: NDVI Time-Series Analysis in Yellowhead Country, AB
text:
NDVI Time-Series Analysis in Yellowhead Country, AB
With NDVI data from the MODIS imagery (MOD13Q1), a time series analysis from 2001 to 2020 was completed. The BFAST change detection algorithm was used to determine NDVI deviation from the expected pattern over two different areas of interest. Examining the evolution of NDVI after each break, allowed informed predictions about the cause of each break to be made.
Check out some code snippets:






Process MODIS imagery and generate NDVI summary





(1)list files
flist <- list.files("data/MOD13Q1_TS",
pattern = "tif$",
full.names = TRUE)

(2)extract date
fname <- basename(flist)
date_ts <- str_extract(fname, pattern = "A[0-9]{7}")
date_ts[1:5]

(3)convert to date format
date_ts <- lubridate::as_date(date_ts, format = "%Y%j")
date_ts[1:5]

(4)make df storing dates
ts <- data.frame(date_ts, year(date_ts))
ts <- rename(ts, c("year" = "year.date_ts."))

(5)summarise number of files per year
ts_summ <- ts %>%
group_by(year) %>%
count(year)

(6)calculate median NDVI of each pixel throughout entire ts
ndvi_ts <- rast(flist)
names(ndvi_ts) <- date_ts
ndvi_ts_med <- app(ndvi_ts,
fun = "median",
na.rm = TRUE)

(7) extract average NDVI for each ROI for each layer in ts
ndvi_roi <- terra::extract(ndvi_ts, vect(aoi), fun = mean, na.rm = TRUE)

(8) create ndvi summary
ndvi_roi_long <- pivot_longer(ndvi_roi, cols = 2:ncol(ndvi_roi), names_to = "name"", values_to = "ndvi")
ndvi_roi_summary <- ndvi_roi_long %>%
filter(name >= ymd("2001-01-01") & name <= ymd("2006-12-19")) %>%
filter(month(name) == 5:9) %>%
group_by(month = month(name)) %>% #add year = year(name), to get year included in summary
summarise(mean_ndvi = mean(ndvi, na.rm = TRUE))









Apply BFAST change detection algorithm





(1)seperate aoi a and b
ndvi_roi_A <- ndvi_roi_long %>%
filter(ID == 1, na.rm = TRUE)
ndvi_roi_B <- ndvi_roi_long %>%
filter(ID == 2, na.rm = TRUE)

(2)convert to ts
A_ts <- ts(ndvi_roi_A$ndvi, frequency = 23, start = 2001-01-01)
B_ts <- ts(ndvi_roi_B$ndvi, frequency = 23, start = 2001-01-01)

(3)run bfast
bfm_A <- bfast::bfastmonitor(A_ts, start = c(2008, 1))
plot(bfm_A)

bfm_B <- bfast::bfastmonitor(B_ts, start = c(2006, 1))
plot(bfm_B)




Visual Results







Change Detection for Area A
A break in expected NDVI pattern occurred in 2009. Due to the sustained decreased NDVI values, a land use change from forest to agriculture was predicted.










Change Detection for Area B
A break in expected NDVI values occurred in 2006. Due to the gradual return of seasonally cyclic NDVI values after the break, a disturbance such as harvesting, fire, or insect outbreak was predicted to cause the change.

title: Time Series Analyses using Satellite Imagery
section: Land Use Change Time-Series Analysis in Williams Lake, BC
text:
Land Use Change Time-Series Analysis in Williams Lake, BC
With Landsat imagery, a time series analysis from 1984 to 2016 was completed to detect changes in land use resulting from forest management. The aim was to examine the evolution if total forested area through time, balancing loss from harvesting and gain from regeneration.
Check out some code snippets:






Process Landsat imagery





(1) list files
flist_vlce <- flist <- list.files("data/VLCE_TS",
pattern = "tif$",
full.names = TRUE)

(2) extract years and rename raster based on their year 
year_ts <- str_extract(flist_vlce, pattern = "[0-9]{4}")
vlce_ts <- rast(flist_vlce)
names(vlce_ts) <- years_ts

(3) visualize first and last year
plot(c(vlce_ts$`1984`, vlce_ts$`2016`)









Quantify and visualize forest gain and loss





(1) reclassify imagery time series into binary forested/non-forested series
lc_reclass[] <- lapply(lc_reclass, as.numeric)
lc_matrix <- as.matrix(lc_reclass) #convert to matrix
lc_matrix <- lc_matrix[ ,2:3] #drop first column
ts_forested <- terra::classify(vlce_ts, lc_matrix, include.lowest = TRUE)

(2) compute difference between years
ts_forested_lag <- terra::diff(ts_forested, lag = 1)

(3) calculate change in forest area
ts_df <- as.data.frame(ts_forested_lag)
df_long <- ts_df %>%
pivot_longer(cols = everything(), names_to = "year", values_to = "value")

#summarise gain and loss per year
cell_area_ha <- 0.09
forest_gain <- df_long %>%
group_by(year) %>%
filter(value == 1) %>%
summarise(n_cells_gain = n()) %>%
mutate(area_gain_ha = n_cells_gain * cell_area_ha)

forest_loss <- df_long %>%
group_by(year) %>%
filter(value == -1) %>%
summarise(n_cells_loss = n()) %>%
mutate(area_loss_ha = n_cells_loss * cell_area_ha)

(5)combine to determine net change
forest_change <- forest_gain %>%
left_join(forest_loss, by = "year") %>%
mutate(net_change_ha = area_gain_ha - area_loss_ha)

#arrange by year and ensure numeric for plotting
forest_change <- forest_change %>%
arrange(year) %>%
mutate(year = as.numeric(year))

(6) plot net change in forest area per year 
ggplot(forest_change, aes(x = year, y = net_change_ha)) +
#geom_point() +
geom_line() +
theme_classic() +
geom_abline(slope = 0, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
labs(
title = "Yearly Net Forest Area Change",
y = "Change in forested area (ha)",
x = "Year") +
scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(min(forest_change$year), max(forest_change$year), by = 1)) +
theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 8))

(7) plot gain and loss over time
#great new column with both changes
total_change <- forest_change %>%
pivot_longer(cols = c(area_gain_ha, area_loss_ha), names_to = "change_type", values_to = "area") %>%
mutate()

ggplot(total_change, aes(x = year, y = area, fill = change_type)) +
geom_col() +
theme_classic() +
scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(min(forest_change$year), max(forest_change$year), by = 1)) +
theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 8)) +
scale_y_symmetric() +
labs(
title = "Yearly Net Forest Area Change",
y = "Change in forested area (ha)",
x = "Year",
fill = "Change Type") +
scale_fill_discrete(labels = c("area_loss_ha" = "Loss", "area_gain_ha" = "Gain"))




Visual Results







Yearly Net Forest Change in Hectares Across Time-Series
Two prominent events of net forest change are seen. Forest gain is largely concentrated in the early 1990’s, while loss is dominated in 2004.










Forest Gain and Loss Quantified Across Time Series
Individual gain and loss shown per year. Prominent gain can be seen in the early 1990’s while prominent loss can be see in the early 2000’s. Year to year variable can be seen throughout.

title: Publications
text:
Peer-reviewed publications:
Despite listing them on your resume, it may also be pertinent to create a seperate tab for publications and reports. As your career progresses, this list may become quite long, so be sure to organize things. You may want to point to ‘most recent’ publications, or categorize things by project/topic.
